coredata.dc:title: Doxorubicin-loaded environmentally friendly carbon dots as a novel drug delivery system for nucleus targeted cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Yifang
Guo, Bin
Hao, Liying
Liu, Na
Lin, Yunfeng
Guo, Wushuang
Li, Xiaoguang
Gu, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Chemotherapy is widely applied against various kinds of carcinoma. Generally, chemotherapeutic agents, such as Doxorubicin (DOX), Paclitaxel (PTX), 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), Methotrexate (MTX), and Vinblastine (VLB) are combined with a view to maximizing their efficacy. Unfortunately, chemotherapeutics are indiscriminate and also kill normal healthy cells, resulting in serious side effects. This non-productive and destructive distribution of chemotherapeutics is regarded as one of the largest problems associated with chemotherapy. Recently, the application of carbon dots (CDs) in cancer therapy has attracted considerable attention due to their attractive properties, such as biocompatibility and low toxicity. We report herein on the fabrication of CD-DOX antitumor drug complexes, from the combination of CDs and DOX, with a view to providing a novel and efficient strategy for cancer treatment. CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of milk, a simple and environmentally friendly synthetic process. DOX was conjugated to the CDs through electrostatic interactions via the multiple surface CD functional groups. The CD-DOX complexes exhibited pH-dependent DOX release behavior. A cytotoxicity study demonstrated that the CDs were non-cytotoxic in the range of concentrations used. Compared to free DOX, the CD-DOX complexes were significantly more destructive to the adenoid cystic carcinoma cell line (ACC-2), but exhibited lower toxicity to a mouse fibroblast cell line (L929). Confocal microscopy and flow cytometry confirmed that CD-DOX complexes increased cancer therapy efficiency through the localization of a much higher quantity of drugs in the nuclei of tumor cells and induced a higher rate of apoptosis in ACC-2 cells, compared to DOX alone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Doxorubicin
Drug delivery
Cancer therapy efficiency

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs and CD-DOX complexes
Cell viability and antitumor efficacy in vitro
Localization and intracellular uptake in ACC-2 cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Images of CDs solution and characteristics of CDs in contact angle, PL, UV–vis and Zeta potential.

Notes: (A) CDs solution in daylights and excited via a 365nm UV light lamp. (B) Hydrophicility of CDs. (C) Emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 340 to 420nm. (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (E) Zeta potential of CDs.
Abbreviations: CDs, carbon dots; PL, photoluminescence; UV–vis, ultraviolet–visible.
Different morphology images of CDs and CDs-DOX in TEM, SEM and AFM.
Notes: (A) TEM images of CDs and CDs-DOX. (B) SEM images of CDs and CDs-DOX. (C) Three-dimensional AFM topography images of CDs and CDs-DOX.
Abbreviations: CDs, carbon dots; DOX, Doxorubicin; TEM, transmission electron microscopy; SEM, scan electron microscopy; AFM, atomic force microscopy.
Characteristics of CDs and CDs-DOX in PL spectra, UV–vis, FT-IR, XPS and XRD.

Notes: (A) PL emission spectra of CDs solution, DOX in aqueous solution and CDs-DOX solution under 360nm excitation. (B) UV–vis absorption of CDs solution, DOX in aqueous solution and CDs-DOX solution. (C) FT-IR spectra of CDs (1), DOX (2) and CDs-DOX (3). (D) XPS spectrum of CDs-DOX and high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. (E) XRD patterns of CDs (1), DOX (2) and CDs-DOX (3).

Abbreviations: CDs, carbon dots; DOX, Doxorubicin; PL, photoluminescence; UV-vis, ultraviolet-visible; FT-IR, Fourier-transform infrared; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy; XRD, X-ray Diffractometer.
pH-sensitive DOX release and cytotoxicity of DOX and CDs-DOX.

Notes: (A) The release curve of CDs-DOX in PBS at different pH value. (B) Cytotoxicity results of CDs to L929 cells via CCK-8 test after incubated at different concentrations for 24h, 48h and 72h. The anticancer activity of free DOX and CDs-DOX to ACC-2 cells (C) or L929 cells (D) after incubated at 1.5μM DOX for 24h, 48h and 72h.
Abbreviations: DOX, Doxorubicin; CDs, carbon dots; PBS, phosphate buffered saline; L929, mouse Mouse fibroblast cell line; CCK-8, Cell Counting Kit-8; ACC-2, human adenoid cystic carcinoma cell.
Confocal microscopy images of ACC-2 cells mixed with CDs, DOX and CDs-DOX for 4h.

Notes: (A) After incubated with CDs (300μgml−1) for 4h, ACC-2 cells were followed by DiI and DAPI staining. (B) Exposed with DOX (3.5μM) for 4h, ACC-2 cells were stained with DiO and DAPI. (C) The cells of CDs-DOX group (at same concentration of DOX) were only stained with DAPI. All groups were analyzed under laser scanning confocal microscope.

Abbreviations: ACC-2, human adenoid cystic carcinoma cell; CDs, carbon dots; DOX, Doxorubicin; DiI, 1,1′-dioctadecyl-3,3,3′,3′-tetramethylindocarbocyanine; DAPI, 4,6-diamino-2-phenyl indole; DiO, 3,3′-dioctadecyloxacarbocyanine perchlorate.
Intracellular uptake of DOX by flow cytometric.
Notes: The ACC-2 cells were incubated with DOX and CDs-DOX for 30min, 1h, 2hand 4h at the same concentration of DOX (1.5μM), followed by flow cytemetry.
Abbreviations: ACC-2, human adenoid cystic carcinoma cell; DOX, Doxorubicin; CDs, carbon dots.
Apoptosis analysis.

Notes: The L929 cells and the ACC-2 cells were incubated with PBS, CDs (200μgml−1), free DOX and CDs-DOX (at the same DOX concentration, 1.5μM) for 48h. Then the cells were stained by PI and Annexin V-FITC, flowed by flow cytometry.
Abbreviations: L929, mouse Mouse fibroblast cell line; ACC-2, human adenoid cystic carcinoma cell; PBS, phosphate buffered saline; CDs, carbon dots; DOX, Doxorubicin; PI, propidium iodide.

head.ce:title: Doxorubicin-loaded environmentally friendly carbon dots as a novel drug delivery system for nucleus targeted cancer therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institution of Stomatology, The General Hospital of China LA, Beijing 100853, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The General Hospital of China PLA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases, National Clinical Research Center for Oral Diseases, West China Hospital of Stomatology, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases
National Clinical Research Center for Oral Diseases

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No.28 Fuxing Road, Beijing, 100853, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Author Contributions: As the co-first author, Yifang Yuan and Bin Guo contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemotherapy is widely applied against various kinds of carcinoma. Generally, chemotherapeutic agents, such as Doxorubicin (DOX), Paclitaxel (PTX), 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), Methotrexate (MTX), and Vinblastine (VLB) are combined with a view to maximizing their efficacy. Unfortunately, chemotherapeutics are indiscriminate and also kill normal healthy cells, resulting in serious side effects. This non-productive and destructive distribution of chemotherapeutics is regarded as one of the largest problems associated with chemotherapy. Recently, the application of carbon dots (CDs) in cancer therapy has attracted considerable attention due to their attractive properties, such as biocompatibility and low toxicity. We report herein on the fabrication of CD-DOX antitumor drug complexes, from the combination of CDs and DOX, with a view to providing a novel and efficient strategy for cancer treatment. CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of milk, a simple and environmentally friendly synthetic process. DOX was conjugated to the CDs through electrostatic interactions via the multiple surface CD functional groups. The CD-DOX complexes exhibited pH-dependent DOX release behavior. A cytotoxicity study demonstrated that the CDs were non-cytotoxic in the range of concentrations used. Compared to free DOX, the CD-DOX complexes were significantly more destructive to the adenoid cystic carcinoma cell line (ACC-2), but exhibited lower toxicity to a mouse fibroblast cell line (L929). Confocal microscopy and flow cytometry confirmed that CD-DOX complexes increased cancer therapy efficiency through the localization of a much higher quantity of drugs in the nuclei of tumor cells and induced a higher rate of apoptosis in ACC-2 cells, compared to DOX alone.

ce:section.ce:para: Current and traditional cancer therapies include surgical intervention, through which tumor tissues can be partially or completely directly eliminated; radiotherapy, which may be used synergistically with chemotherapy or alone for localized therapies, or as an adjuvant to surgical therapy; and chemotherapy, which is widely applied as a pre-operative or post-operative adjuvant therapy or for carcinomas which may be unsuitable for surgical treatment, and is characterized by its ability to work throughout the body rather than in a localized area alone [1,2]. However, chemotherapeutics present many disadvantages such as low therapeutic efficiency and high non-productive drug distribution, which leads to the application of high drug doses to achieve adequate therapeutic results and undesirable side effects during treatment [3]. With a view to overcoming these significant issues, novel drug delivery systems that exhibit excellent properties for the loading, release, tracking, and targeting of chemotherapeutic drugs, are drawing ever-increasing amounts of interest in cancer diagnosis and treatment [4–7]. A number of drug delivery systems are and have been developed whose specific design aid in the targeted delivery of the anticancer agent. For example, these delivery vehicles may possess surface functional groups that facilitate the carriage of diverse agents through electrostatic interactions or covalent bonding [8,9]. Other systems have been designed to enable the slow release of drugs after the systems enter the tumor, rather than fast premature release outside of the target zone [10]. Some have been constructed to include fluorescent labels to enable the tracking of the delivery system, which may help elucidate the mechanisms behind the chemotherapeutic action [11]. Other drug delivery candidates endeavor to avoid distribution of the toxic drugs to normal cells by linking to some special cancer-targeted biomolecules [12].

Recent years have seen the development of nanotechnology-based cancer therapeutics move at quite a fast pace with the recruitment of graphene, quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, dendrimers, liposomes, and nanoparticles as drug delivery agents [13,14]. Notably, studies on graphene oxide in drug delivery are numerous and its success is attributed to its excellent thermal, electronic, and mechanical properties and its high drug loading capacity. Many of these nanomaterials can be synthesized from biodegradable materials that exhibit good biocompatibility and their subcellular size facilitates good intracellular uptake or endocytosis [6,15]. Many have been nanodesigned to target cancerous cells or tumors after functionalization with appropriate biomolecules or pH-, photo-, magnetic- and temperature-sensitive carriers [15–17]. Furthermore, studies have shown that nanomaterials can stimulate cell growth to promote proliferation or differentiation by acting as nanoscaffolds that mimic the in vivo environment [18,19].

Generally, quantum dots exhibit high aqueous solubility, intense photoluminescence (PL), high resistance to photobleaching, excellent chemical inertness, and lend themselves to facile functionalization. These favorable properties have resulted in wide-spread research interest and their practical application in the fields of cell imaging and drug delivery. [20–22]. Core-shell quantum dots (CdSe-dots) and Mn/ZnS quantum dots have been used for the fluorescence imaging of cancer cells. However, the synthetic production of these quantum dots is complicated and the raw materials are challenging to source [23,24]. Above all, these heavy metals present potential danger to humans. Carbon quantum dots (CDs), are mainly comprised of carbon and can be prepared by a green synthetic strategy using nontoxic chemicals, including environmentally friendly solvents [25–31]. Researchers first discovered CDs during the electrophoretic fractionation of arc-discharge soot in 2004 [32]. Since then, various patented and green synthetic methods have been used for their preparation. Liu obtained CDs by hydrothermal treatment of grass, using natural plants as the carbon source to replace conventional chemicals [25]. Wang used the hydrothermal treatment of milk, an economical and renewable material, to develop N-doped CDs [26]. Wang synthesized CDs via microwave-assisted pyrolysis of eggshell membrane ashes; Mojtaba prepared CDs through the hydrothermal treatment of salep flour [27,28]. All of these methods resulted in the fabrication of CDs that exhibited the favorable properties of low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Doxorubicin (DOX) is a commonly used anti-cancer drug, which is highly regarded in cancer therapy due to its broad spectrum efficacy. However, like most chemotherapeutics, the intracellular uptake of DOX is mainly by passive diffusion, and it shows low affinity to cell nuclei, which results in relatively low cancer therapy efficacy, when applied alone.

CDs have shown good promise in drug delivery. Beack used transdermal carbon dot-chlorine e6-hyaluronate (Cdot-Ce6-HA) conjugates for the treatment of melanoma skin cancer, which exhibited a favorable antitumor effect in an in vivo model [33]. A study by Wang showed that CDs loaded with DOX inhibited HeLa cell growth [34]. However, studies concerning CD-DOX conjugates are rare and their development is in its relative infancy [34]. Gong used phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dots (PNHCDs) for the targeted in vivo delivery of DOX with biological imaging capacity [35]. Sachdev generated chitosan based polymeric hydrogels composed of CDs loaded with anticancer drugs for the targeted induction of apoptosis in lung cancer cells [36]. Yang coupled DOX to nuclear localization signal peptide-CDs, which induced apoptosis in A549 cells and exhibited a superior capacity to inhibit cancer than free DOX in an A549 xenograft nude mice model [37]. As a carbon nanomaterial like graphene, CDs possess remarkable properties in terms of mechanics, chemistry, and biological potential.

In this study, we applied CDs for anticancer drug delivery using DOX as the model chemotherapeutic drug, with a view to improving the therapeutic efficiency of DOX. The CDs were fabricated through the thermolysis of milk and electrostatic interactions between the surface of the CDs and the DOX facilitated complexation to produce CD-DOX complexes [34,37]. This practical strategy boasts a lot of advantages including an environmentally friendly, economical, and non-toxic synthetic process [26]. For DOX loading, the CDs and DOX were incubated in PBS at pH 7.4. The CD-DOX complexes were thoroughly characterized through morphological, physical, and chemical studies. Assessment of drug localization, cellular uptake, and therapeutic efficiency of the CD-DOX system was achieved using the L929 mouse fibroblast cell line and the ACC-2 adenoid cystic carcinoma cell line. Finally, a comparative analysis of apoptosis induced by free DOX and CD mediated released DOX was performed through biochemical detection.

Milk used in this experiment was obtained from a supermarket in Chengdu, China. Deionized water (18.25MΩcm−1) was obtained from an ULUPURE water purification system. DOX was purchased from TRC-Canada, Toronto, Canada. The syringe filter, dialysis bag (MWCO=1000Da), and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Sigma Alrich (Missouri, United States). The mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) and human adenoid cystic carcinoma cell line (ACC-2) were obtained from the Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases of Sichuan University (Chengdu, China). RPMI 1640 medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin streptomycin, and a humidified incubator (5% CO2, 37°C; Binder, Germany) were used to incubate the L929 cells and ACC-2 cells (the Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases of Sichuan University, China). The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) and Annexin V-FITC Apoptosis Detection Kit I were purchased from the Dojinda Laboratory, Japan. For the cellular uptake observation experiments, 4,6-diamino-2-phenyl indole (DAPI), 3,3′-dioctadecyloxacarbocyanine perchlorate (DiO) and 1,1′-dioctadecyl-3,3,3′,3′-tetramethylindocarbocyanine (DiI), were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology, United States.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and CD-DOX complexes
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of milk [26]. Briefly, 20mL of milk was first added to 16mL of deionized water and stirred until thoroughly mixed. The mixed solution was transferred to a 50mL stainless steel autoclave vessel and was heated to 180°C for 2h. This heat treatment changed the color of the mixture from a white solution to a brown one. The product was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally before being centrifuged at 12,000rpm using a centrifuge (Cence, China) for 30min. The centrifuged liquid was subsequently filtered through syringe filters (pore size 0.22μm), to remove solid particles and obtain pure CDs [26,28]. The brown CD solution was maintained at 4°C until needed for further experimentation [25–31].

For complex formation, 1mL of CD solution (8mgmL−1) and 1mL of DOX solution (400μgmL−1) were mixed with PBS at pH 7.4 under stirring to form a final solution system of 4mL. This solution was then allowed to react for 24h on an incubator shaker (Crystal, China), set to 200rpm and 24°C in the dark [34]. The resultant solution was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (MWCO=1000Da) against 40mL deionized water for 2h to remove any unreacted DOX or CDs. The CD-DOX solution was maintained at 4°C in the dark, until needed for further experiments.

Fluorescence emission spectral measurements were carried out using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC, Japan). The excitation wavelength was increased by 10nm increments from 340nm to 420nm.
Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired by a Hitachi U-3900H spectrometer.
A drop of the CD solution was applied to a glass slide and then air-dried to form a thin membrane. The hydrophilicity of the CDs was assessed using a contact angle meter (XG-CAMB; Shanghai, China).

To measure the zeta potential, the CD solution was diluted and injected into a folded capillary supported with platinum electrodes, and placed in the sample holder of a Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, England) for analysis.

Diluted CD and CD-DOX solutions, at a relatively high concentration, were dropped onto the non-shiny sides of carbon-coated copper TEM grids and left to dry. Morphological examination of the samples was operated through a FEI Technai G220 TEM (Netherlands), at 200kV.

Dilutions of the CDs and CD-DOX solutions were dropped onto circular slides (Φ=10mm) and air-dried. These were then sputter coated with gold to enable visualization under SEM. The CDs and CD-DOX complexes were observed under an INCA PentaFETx3 SEM (Oxford, England), at an operating voltage of 5kV.

Diluted solutions of CDs and CD-DOX complexes were prepared and dropped onto circle slides (Φ=10mm). Images of these samples were acquired by a multimode atomic force microscope (Shimadzu, Japan). Then, a SPM-9700 accessory was used for analysis.
FTIR spectra of the products were acquired using a Spark CCD 6000 spectrometer (Thermo, Japan). The samples were prepared as powders for detection.
CDs, DOX, and CD-DOX complexes were air-dried and crushed to form powders, XPS spectra were acquired from an ESCALAB-MKII XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA).

The crystallinity of the prepared products was analyzed by XRD. CDs, DOX, and CD-DOX solutions at high concentrations were dropped onto glass slides and then dried in an oven at 55°C. The prepared samples were examined under a XRD spectrometer (Advance, Germany).

DOX loading efficiency was assessed by fluorescence spectrophotometery at a wavelength of 485nm and calculated according to a calibration curve. [37].The curve was previously graphed using various concentrations of DOX solutions. After preparing the CD and DOX mixture in buffer, as described in Section 2.2 the solutions were dialyzed against deionized water for 2h. After this time, the solution inside the dialysis bag was CD-DOX complexes. The unconjugated DOX was dialyzed into the deionized water, outside of the dialysis bag. The loading efficiency (DLE) and loading content (DLC) of DOX could be calculated according to the following equations:
(1) DLE (%)=(ADOX −Afree)/ADOX ×100%
(2) DLC (mgg−1)=(ADOX −Afree)/ACDs
ADOX denotes the total amount of DOX, Afree refers to the amount of DOX dialyzed into the deionized water, and ACDs represents the quantity of CDs used in the loading experiment.

The pH-dependent drug release in vitro experiment was performed on a shaking table at 200rpm at 24°C, in dark conditions. Briefly, 1mL aliquots of the CD-DOX solution were dialyzed using a dialysis bag (MWCO=1000Da) against 10mL of PBS, across a range of different pH values (pH 7.4, 6.8, 5.0). These were chosen to simulate the different environments of human tissues (physiological environment, interstitial fluid of cancer, endochylema of cancer cells) [38]. After certain time intervals on the shaking table, aliquots of the PBS solutions were removed and the concentration of the released DOX was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometery, as described previously.
L929 cells and ACC-2 cells were first seeded in RPMI 1640 medium, containing 10% FBS and 1% penicillin streptomycin, in a 5% CO2, 37°C humidified incubator.

Once the cells had proliferated to 80% confluence, they were harvested for experimentation. The L929 cells and ACC-2 cells were counted and seeded in 96-well plates, containing 200μL of complete medium, at a density of 104 cells per well. The plates were then reincubated overnight.

The L929 cells were then incubated with CDs at concentrations of 100μgmL−1, 200μgmL−1, 300μgmL−1, 400μgmL−1, and 600μgmL−1, and reincubated for another 24h, 48h, and 72h to examine the cytotoxicity of the CDs.

Free DOX or CD-DOX complexes (at the same DOX concentration, 1.5μM) were added into culture wells of both L929 cells and ACC-2 cells, and cultured for 24h, 48h, and 72h, to compare the cytotoxicities of DOX and CD-DOX. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS to remove all cell culture medium in the 96-well plates. All cell wells were enriched with a 100μL solution, which contained 10% CCK-8 reagent and 90% RPMI 1640 medium. Then, the cells were incubated for 2h in the humidified incubator in the dark. Finally, the cell viability was estimated using a microplate reader (VariOskanFlas 3001, Thermo, USA) at a wavelength of 450nm.

ACC-2 cells at a density of 105 cells per well were seeded in three Petri dishes (Φ=35mm) and incubated overnight, specifically for laser scanning confocal microscopy. Then, the cells in the three Petri dishes were incubated with CDs (300μgmL−1), DOX, and CD-DOX complexes (using the same DOX concentration, 3.5μM) respectively for 4h. Afterwards, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove residual medium. Later, the cells were fixed with 1.5mL 4% paraformaldehyde for 15min and washed with PBS three times. The cells incubated with the CDs were stained with 6μM DiI for 15min followed by rinsing with PBS, three times (for 5min every time). They were subsequently stained with 1μgmL−1 DAPI for 15min. To the cells incubated with DOX, 6μM DiO for was added for 15min followed by 1μgmL−1 DAPI for another 15min. Then, these cells were similarly washed three times with PBS. The cells incubated with the CD-DOX complexes were only stained with 1μgmL−1 DAPI for 15min. All these staining and washing steps were completed on a shaking table in dark conditions. Finally, 1mL 10% glycerinum was added to each dish, and they were stored at 4°C. The intracellular localization of CDs, DOX, and the CD-DOX complexes was observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy.

Flow cytometry was used for analyzing cellular uptake of free DOX and CD-DOX complexes in ACC-2 cells. ACC-2 cells were seeded in eight small Petri dishes (Φ=35mm) at a density of 2×105 cells per well, and incubated overnight. After that, free DOX solution was added into four of the Petri dishes and CD-DOX solution at the same DOX concentration (1.5μM) was added into the other four. The cells, exposed to free DOX or CD-DOX, were incubated for different times (30min, 1h, 2h, and 4h). Then, the cells were centrifuged at 1000rpm for 5min and washed twice with PBS to remove medium. The collected cells were dispersed in 500μL PBS for flow cytometry analysis (Beckman Coulter Cytomics FC 500, USA).

L929 cells and ACC-2 cells were seeded in 6-well plates at a density of 2×105 cells per well and incubated overnight. Then, the cells were incubated with either PBS (control), CDs (200μgmL−1), DOX (1.5μM), or CD-DOX (at the same DOX concentration) for 48h. Afterwards, the cells were centrifuged at 1000rpm for 5min and washed with PBS three times. After the cells were suspended in 100μL 1×AnnexinV Binding Solution, they were stained with 5μL Annexin V-FITC and 5μL propidium iodide (PI) for 15min, in dark conditions. The percentages of apoptotic cells in each group was quantified by flow cytometry.

Data were analyzed via the Student’s t-test using GraphPad Prism 6.0 and values represented by *(p<0.05), **(p<0.005), ***(p<0.0005), and ****(p<0.0001) were considered significant. Quantitative experiments were repeated at least three times with at least three samples in each group.

In this work, CDs were generated as a brown solution using milk and water by a hydrothermal treatment method, as previously reported [26]. Milk, healthy and almost universally used in daily life, served as the carbon source due to its carbon and nitrogen rich nature. Optical images of the CD solution under natural and ultraviolet light (365nm) are shown in Fig. 1 A. Brown CDs exhibited intense green fluorescence under UV light. Unlike heavy metal containing quantum dots (CdSe-dots and Mn/ZnS dots), CDs are more environmentally friendly and biocompatible, and are therefore more biosecure. Compared with conventional organic dyes, the fluorescence of CDs is stable and uneasy to quench, which is essential for the observation of drug release processes [39–41]. The contact angle of the CDs was 10.4 °, demonstrating that the fabricated CDs were highly hydrophilic (Fig. 1B). Furthermore, the CDs were not only highly water soluble, they were also stable in a variety of solutions. Fig. 1F presents images of CDs dissolved in four different kinds of solution. All of the solutions, water, PBS, and RPMI 1640 medium without and with FBS, were limpid after the CDs had been present for one day. The same was true even after three days, Fig. 2D,E. The four solutions all remained clear and no tendency to precipitate was observed. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CD solutions were captured at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 340nm to 420nm, with increasing increments of 10nm, Fig. 1C. The CDs showed the largest PL intensity at a maximum emission peak at about 444nm when excited at 360nm. The PL intensity increased gradually when CDs were excited from 320nm to 360nm, before decreasing gradually when the excitation wavelengths exceeded 360nm. Moreover, the PL spectra of the CDs presented an excitation-dependent emission behavior [42,43]. When the excitation wavelengths increased from 340nm to 420nm, the emission peaks moved to longer wavelengths (red-shifted). As reported before, this is a typical characteristic of CDs [20,28,41]. Fig. 1D shows the UV–vis spectrum recorded for the CDs, the CDs display a broad and obvious absorption spectrum in the UV visible range from 270nm to 400nm, with an especially strong peak evident at approximately 290nm. This is consistent with other reports, and is a familiar characteristic of CDs that indicates π-π conjugations in the CDs, which can be attributed to the carbonic core center of the nanocarbon particles [41,44,45]. The CDs exhibited a zeta potential value of −7.38mV (Fig. 1E), which again is characteristic of CDs. This result can be ascribed to the existence of oxygen and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of these particles, which is further probed in the results and discussion later [46]. Therefore, the negatively charged CDs should be able to link with drugs by electrostatic interaction.

TEM, SEM and AFM were employed in our study to observe the morphology of the fabricated CDs and CD-DOX complexes. Fig. 2 A shows the TEM images of both. The left image of CDs highlights their small nature, less than 10nm. The larger particles were regarded as CDs that had not been separated from each other. CD-DOX complexes, right hand image, exhibited a spherical morphology with a relatively larger size of approximately 20nm. Fig. 2B shows the SEM images of CDs and CD-DOX complexes in high coverage. The structure of these particles was further studied by AFM analysis. Fig. 2C shows the AFM constructed images of the CDs and CD-DOX complexes, illustrating the relative height of these particles. The CDs had an average height distribution of 1.3nm (Fig. 2D,E), and the average height of disperse CD-DOX complexes was approximately 4nm (Fig. 2D,E). The recorded size and morphology of the CDs are consistent with results reported previously [47].

CDs, DOX, and CD-DOX complexes were excited at 360nm, Fig. 3 A. CDs emitted a large PL intensity, with a maximum emission peak recorded at approximately 450nm. Two emission peaks of free DOX can be seen at nearly 550nm and 600nm. CD-DOX complexes displayed two emission peaks but had a blue shift, confirming the complexation [37]. In the UV–vis absorption spectra, presented in Fig. 3B, free DOX exhibited a maximum peak at about 490nm. In the CD trace from 425nm to 600nm, the trace is barely discernible. However, CD-DOX spectra displayed two typical peaks at 275nm and 485nm, also with a blue shift, further confirming the fabrication of the CD-DOX complexes. This blue shift, characteristic of CD-DOX, has been previously reported [37]. Fig. 3C shows the overlapping FT-IR traces of the CDs, DOX, and CD-DOX complexes. Generally, the absorption bands at 3400cm−1 corresponded to OH and NH stretching vibrations; peaks appearing at 2900cm−1 can be assigned to CH stretching; the typical absorption band of CO stretching vibrations can be observed around 1600cm−1; the peak at 1200cm−1 is characteristic of CN stretching vibrations; and bands appearing at 1100cm−1 can be assigned to COC in stretching mode [26,28]. For the CDs, the absorption peaks suggest the existence of carboxylic acid, hydroxyl groups, oxygen and nitrogen functional groups. These functional groups give CDs their hydrophilic quality. Indeed, the presence of these many functional groups point to the flexibility of the CDs, which is essential for attaching DOX through electrostatic interaction. The shift of wavenumbers and the extra absorption peaks observed for CD-DOX at 1200cm−1 compared to the free DOX spectrum further confirm the successful fabrication of the CD-DOX complexes. XPS in Fig. 3D provided information concerning the elemental composition of CD-DOX, which revealed the presence of C, N, and O in the complexes, with some small inorganic elements due to the CD-DOX synthesis while attaining a solution system at pH 7.4 with PBS. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s was deconvoluted into five peaks with binding energies of 287.6, 286.2, 285.5, 284.7, and 284.1eV, attributed to CO, CN, CO, CC/CH, and CC bands, respectively. The N 1s spectrum showed two components with binding energies of 400.0 and 399.3eV, assigned to N-(C)3 and CCN groups [48,49]. The spectrum of O 1s indicated two peaks at binding energies of 532.5 and 531.2eV, corresponding to COH/CO-C and CO bands. The functional bands of CN and CCN in the spectra gives further evidence of successful complex conjugation. The single broad peak displayed in Fig. 3E, centered at approximately 23° in the XRD pattern, highlights the amorphous nature of all of the products.

Briefly, for the CD-DOX complex synthesis, 1mL of CDs (8mgmL−1) and 1mL DOX solution (400μgmL−1) were mixed at pH 7.4 and reacted for 24h, to obtain CD-DOX complexes. The resulting solution was dialyzed for 2h and unreacted DOX passed into 40mL of deionized water. According to the DOX PL intensity calibration curve (excited at 485nm), the concentration of unreacted DOX dialyzed into the deionized water was 3.23×10−2 μgmL−1. The DLE and DLC of DOX in this loading experiment was then calculated. The total amount of DOX was 100μg; the amount of unreacted DOX was approximately 13μg; the quantity of CDs was 2mg. Therefore, DLE was about 87%, and DLC was about 43.5mgg−1. The possible mechanisms of DOX-loading can be explained by the characteristics of the CDs. On the one hand, there are several functional and flexible groups on the CD surfaces that are conducive to the formation of bonds between the CDs and DOX. On the other hand, the DOX was positively charged (pH 7.4), while zeta potential measurements revealed that the CDs were negatively charged. Hence, DOX can be loaded to the surface of CDs by means of electrostatic interaction [39]. Moreover, the excellent hydrophilicity of the CDs facilitates DOX attaching through hydrogen bonding.

For the DOX release experiment, pH-dependent release behavior was observed, Fig. 4 A. Briefly, CD-DOX solutions were dialyzed against PBS solutions of different pH character (pH 7.4, 6.8, 5.0). The highest quantity of DOX released was at pH 5.0, which indicated that CD-DOX complexes have the capacity to selectively release in acidic environments. It is well known that the endochylema of cancer cells is approximately pH 5.0 and normal physiological environments are approximately pH 7.4. This CD-DOX property is clearly highly advantageous in terms of promoting the efficiency of anticancer therapy. Moreover, the release of DOX continued slowly for up to 72h, which indicates that the DOX release rate from CD-DOX complexes is relatively slow. These results indicate that the established system not only improved the site selectivity of the drug release, but also is conducive to prolonged drug release, and therefore can extend the therapeutic effect period in a target specific manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability and antitumor efficacy in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The measured in vivo cytotoxicity of the CDs is shown in Fig. 4B. The CDs were non-cytotoxic to L929 cells, even at a high concentration of 600μgmL−1, over a 24h incubation time. When the exposure time was extended to 48 and 72h, the presence of CDs resulted in a slight drop in cell viability to 91.5% and 90%, respectively. These results reveal that within the wide concentration range studied, and with incubation for up to 72h, the cell viability was higher than 90% in all cases. Therefore, it can be concluded that the CDs showed low-cytotoxicity to L929 cells and were nearly non-cytotoxic in concentrations considered to be reasonable for practical application. Interestingly, the cell viability was over 100%, significantly higher than the control group, when the concentration of CDs was approximately 200 or 300μgmL−1, which indicates that the CDs at low concentrations may in fact promote proliferation. Numerous publications highlight that nanomaterials may stimulate cell growth and promote proliferation or differentiation by structuring scaffolds [18]. This property and its consequences will be studied in further work. Meanwhile, the cytotoxicity of free DOX and CD-DOX complexes was also examined by CCK-8. ACC-2 cells were incubated with 1.5μM DOX and CD-DOX complexes (at the same concentration of DOX) for 24h, 48h and 72h, Fig. 4C. It can be clearly observed that the survival rates of ACC-2 cells at all time points in the CD-DOX group were significantly lower than in the free DOX group. When the exposure time reached 72h, the cell viability of the CD-DOX group was only about 5%. The difference observed for the cytotoxicity of CD-DOX and free DOX (at the same DOX concentration) to ACC-2 cells, strongly suggests that CD-DOX complexes may have higher antitumor efficiency than free DOX. Notably, in a comparative experiment using L929 cells, DOX, a common antitumor chemotherapeutic agent, exhibited lower cytotoxicity to normal cells than cancer cells in both groups, which could be ascribed to the selectivity of DOX, Fig. 4D [50].When exposed to free DOX and CD-DOX complexes over the same time points, the survival rates of L929 cells in the CD-DOX group were significantly higher than the free DOX group, which can probably be attributed to the slow release of DOX from the complexes. Consequently, the cytotoxicity of the CD-DOX complexes was much lower for DOX-insensitive L929 cells than free DOX. However, even with the slow release rate of DOX, CD-DOX complexes displayed a higher cytotoxicity to ACC-2 cells than free DOX. These results must be relevant to the mechanism of the CD-DOX complex drug carrier action and future work will further probe this selectivity phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Localization and intracellular uptake in ACC-2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular uptake of CDs, free DOX, and CD-DOX complexes was monitored by confocal laser scanning microscopy and flow cytometry. Fig. 5 shows confocal fluorescence images of ACC-2 cells that had been incubated with CDs (300μgmL−1), free DOX (3.5μM), and CD-DOX (at the same concentration of DOX) for 4h. CDs were predominantly located in the cytoplasm with a smaller quantity visible in the nucleus. Free DOX was located both in the nucleus and cytoplasm but was mainly concentrated at the nuclear membranes. Notably, the CD-DOX complexes showed distinct nuclear localization, which was in contrast to the CDs and free DOX. This targeted delivery to the nucleus is essential to maximize the therapeutic impact of DOX in an antitumor strategy, as DOX needs to reach the nucleus to exert its impact. These results are in line with the previously outlined CCK-8 results that showed CD-DOX complexes to be more cytotoxic than free DOX.

Flow cytometry was used for the quantification of intracellular uptake of free DOX and CD-DOX complexes into ACC-2 cells (in Fig. 6 ). The data indicates that the relative cell uptake of free DOX was higher than the CD-DOX complexes, over the same incubation times. Free DOX had accumulated in every cell 4h after incubation. The comparative accumulation was distinctly lower for the CD-DOX complexes when ACC-2 cells were incubated with complexes for 0.5h, 1h and 2h, than free DOX. However, in the CD-DOX group, a considerable increase in cell count was observed after an exposure time of 4h. The flow cytometry procedure sought to obtain a count of cells that internalized the model drug by measuring the fluorescence of DOX. Therefore the disparity between the fluorescence of cells that had been incubated with DOX and CD-DOX may be due to the slow release profile of DOX from the CD-DOX complexes, as unreleased DOX might not be detected [51]. This explanation would account for the lower count observed for up to 2h incubation and the obvious rise after 4h.

The intracellular uptake of free DOX is through passive diffusion, while evidence shows that CDs should be transported into cells through both endocytosis and passive diffusion [37,52]. Although we cannot provide evidence of increased uptake of DOX through complexation with the CDs by flow cytometry, the combination of the two pathways might explain the possibility.

Flow cytometry was also used to quantify the impact of free DOX and CD-DOX complexes on cell apoptosis in both L929 and ACC-2 cells, the results of which are detailed in Fig. 7 . The cells were sorted into four states: Q1 referred to necrotic cells; Q2 late apoptotic cells; Q3 early apoptotic cells; Q4 intact cells.

L929 and ACC-2 cells were incubated respectively with PBS (control), CDs (200μgmL−1), free DOX (1.5μM), and CD-DOX (at the same DOX concentration) for 48h. The percentage of intact cells in the control and CDs groups for both cell types exceeded 90%, which confirmed the low cytotoxicity of the CDs. For free DOX treated L929 cells, the percentage of intact cells was 80%. The comparative L929 result was a little higher in the CD-DOX group at 86.2%, which was consistent with the results obtained by CCK-8. The percentage of apoptotic cells was higher than the percentage of necrotic cells in both the free DOX and CD-DOX groups for the L929 cells. In the free DOX group of ACC-2 cells, the fraction of apoptotic cells was about 5% while the percentage of necrotic cells was as high as 43%. The ratio of apoptosis to necrosis was nearly 1–9. In the ACC-2 CD-DOX group, the fraction of apoptosis was about 21.3% and the percentage necrotic cells was 67.6%, i.e. the ratio was approximately 1–3. Therefore, for the cancer cells, incubation with the CD-DOX complexes not only resulted in a higher degree of cell death, but also a higher degree of apoptosis, in comparison to free DOX. According to a previous study, a lower percentage of necrotic cells is conductive to promoting the therapeutic effect and weakening the toxicity of antitumor drugs [37]. These results indicate that CDs are excellent drug delivery agents, which should increase the antitumor efficacy of anticancer drugs by nuclear localization, as described earlier, and the apoptotic pathway.

In this work, milk hydrothermolysis was used to obtain pure CDs, and DOX was conjugated with the CDs through electrostatic interactions. The physical and chemical character of the CDs and CD-DOX complexes was thoroughly investigated using a variety of analytical techniques such as TEM, AFM, PL, FT-IR, XPS, and XRD. The observed differences between the CDs and the CD-DOX complexes confirm the successful preparation of the CD-DOX complexes. The pH-dependent DOX release efficiency of the drug delivery system was examined in vitro at pH 5.0, 6.8 and 7.4, in an effort to mimic the environment of the human body. According to the CCK-8 assay, CDs alone were non-cytotoxic to L929 cells at reasonable concentrations. CD-DOX complexes meanwhile exhibited enhanced antitumor behavior toward ACC-2 cells and reduced cytotoxicity to DOX-insensitive L929 cells. Confocal scanning microscopy confirmed the nucleus focused accumulation of CD-DOX complexes. Furthermore, the flow cytometry results further supported the observed superior anticancer capacity of CD-DOX. The complexes, which caused a higher level of apoptosis and lower level of necrosis than free DOX when incubated with ACC-2 cells, show excellent promise as prospective anticancer agents. In summary, this newly designed drug delivery system not only offers a synthetic method which is more environmentally friendly than more traditional approaches, the resultant drug delivery vehicles show high specificity and selectivity for cancer cells, and their application in cancer therapeutics and mechanism of action is worthy of further study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31670998 and 51473175), and the Beijing Nova program (Z141107001814101).
